questions.prompt: Why verify JWTs locally instead of calling an external auth API per request?

choices.text: Local verification is fast (microseconds via Web Crypto) and free; external API calls add 50-200ms latency per request and cost money / rate limits. JWTs are designed to be self-validating — the signature proves the token came from the issuer; the payload contains claims (user ID, expiration, etc.) you can trust if signature is valid. For HMAC (HS256), you have a shared secret. For RSA/ECDSA (RS256), fetch JWKS once and cache (refresh hourly or longer). Hono's jwt middleware handles both patterns. Reserve external API calls for cases where you need real-time info beyond the JWT (current permissions, account status) — and even then, cache aggressively.

questions.explanation: Local crypto verification is microseconds vs 50-200ms external API. JWTs designed for this.

questions.prompt: App uses session cookies. What cookie attributes are essential for security?

choices.text: Set: `httpOnly: true` (prevents JS access; XSS protection — even if attacker injects script, can't steal session cookie), `secure: true` (HTTPS-only; prevents man-in-the-middle on insecure networks), `sameSite: 'Lax'` (blocks most CSRF — cookie sent on top-level navigations but not on cross-site subresource requests), `path: '/'` (cookie applies to all routes), reasonable `maxAge`. For cross-origin auth: `sameSite: 'None'` + `secure: true` (None requires secure). Pair with CSRF middleware (hono/csrf) if cookie-based and serving forms. Skipping any of these creates real vulnerabilities — a non-httpOnly cookie is the most common 'stolen session' attack vector.

questions.explanation: httpOnly + secure + sameSite='Lax'. Each blocks specific attack categories.
